smu.name: OCsmu
smu.title: Soil Map Units
smu.description: This feature layer displays soil map units of the Orange County, California. It is derived from the US Department of Agriculture, Natural Resources Conservation Service SSURGO dataset.
smu.phenomenon: Soils of the United States and associated territories

shg.name: OCshg
shg.title: Soil Hydrologic Group
shg.description: This layer displays hydrologic soil group in seven classes that describe the rate that the soil absorbs rainfall for Orange County, California. It is derived from the US Department of Agriculture, Natural Resources Conservation Service SSURGO dataset, updated by Esri annually.
shg.data_coordinate_system: WKID 5070 USA Contiguous Albers Equal Area Conic USGS version

classes.Group A: Group A soils consist of deep, well drained sands or gravelly sands with high infiltration and low runoff rates.
classes.Group B: Group B soils consist of deep well drained soils with a moderately fine to moderately coarse texture and a moderate rate of infiltration and runoff.
classes.Group C: Group C consists of soils with a layer that impedes the downward movement of water or fine textured soils and a slow rate of infiltration.
classes.Group D: Group D consists of soils with a very slow infiltration rate and high runoff potential. This group is composed of clays that have a high shrink-swell potential, soils with a high water table, soils that have a clay pan or clay layer at or near the surface, and soils that are shallow over nearly impervious material.
classes.Group A/D: Group A/D soils naturally have a very slow infiltration rate due to a high water table but will have high infiltration and low runoff rates if drained.
classes.Group B/D: Group B/D soils naturally have a very slow infiltration rate due to a high water table but will have a moderate rate of infiltration and runoff if drained.
classes.Group C/D: Group C/D soils naturally have a very slow infiltration rate due to a high water table but will have a slow rate of infiltration if drained.

salb.name: OCsalb
salb.title: Soil Albedo
salb.description: This layer displays the ratio of the amount of solar energy absorbed and reflected by the soil for Orange County, California. It is derived from the US Department of Agriculture, Natural Resources Conservation Service SSURGO dataset, updated by Esri annually.
salb.data_coordinate_system: WKID 5070 USA Contiguous Albers Equal Area Conic USGS version

sef.name: OCsef
sef.title: Soil Erodibility Factor
sef.description: Soil erodibility factor quantifies the susceptibility of soils to erosion and is used as a key input to the Universal Soil Loss Equation and other erosion models for Orange County, California. It is derived from the US Department of Agriculture, Natural Resources Conservation Service SSURGO dataset, updated by Esri annually.
sef.phenomenon: Erodibility factor (not adjusted for rock fragments)
sef.data_coordinate_system: WKID 5070 USA Contiguous Albers Equal Area Conic USGS version

saws.name: OCsaws
saws.title: Soil Available Water Storage (0-150 cm)
saws.description: This layer displays the amount of water that the upper 150 cm of soil can store and is available to plants for Orange County, California. It is derived from the US Department of Agriculture, Natural Resources Conservation Service SSURGO dataset, updated by Esri annually.
saws.alias: OC Soil Available Water Storage (0-150 cm)
saws.phenomenon: Amount of water a soil can hold that is available to plants
saws.data_coordinate_system: WKID 5070 USA Contiguous Albers Equal Area Conic USGS version

sdc.name: OCsdc
sdc.title: Soil Drainage Class
sdc.description: This layer classifies soils in seven classes based on the rate that water moves through the soil for Orange County, California. It is derived from the US Department of Agriculture, Natural Resources Conservation Service SSURGO dataset, updated by Esri annually.
sdc.data_coordinate_system: WKID 5070 USA Contiguous Albers Equal Area Conic USGS version

classes.Excessively drained: Water is removed very rapidly. The occurrence of internal free water commonly is very rare or very deep. The soils are commonly coarse-textured and have very high hydraulic conductivity or are very shallow.
classes.Somewhat excessively drained: Water is removed from the soil rapidly. Internal free water occurrence commonly is very rare or very deep. The soils are commonly coarse-textured and have high saturated hydraulic conductivity or are very shallow.
classes.Well drained: Water is removed from the soil readily but not rapidly. Internal free water occurrence commonly is deep or very deep; annual duration is not specified. Water is available to plants throughout most of the growing season in humid regions. Wetness does not inhibit growth of roots for significant periods during most growing seasons. The soils are mainly free of the deep to redoximorphic features that are related to wetness.
classes.Moderately well drained: Water is removed from the soil somewhat slowly during some periods of the year. Internal free water occurrence commonly is moderately deep and transitory through permanent. The soils are wet for only a short time within the rooting depth during the growing season, but long enough that most mesophytic crops are affected. They commonly have a moderately low or lower saturated hydraulic conductivity in a layer within the upper 1 m, periodically receive high rainfall, or both.
classes.Somewhat poorly drained: Water is removed slowly so that the soil is wet at a shallow depth for significant periods during the growing season. The occurrence of internal free water commonly is shallow to moderately deep and transitory to permanent. Wetness markedly restricts the growth of mesophytic crops, unless artificial drainage is provided. The soils commonly have one or more of the following characteristics: low or very low saturated hydraulic conductivity, a high water table, additional water from seepage, or nearly continuous rainfall.
classes.Poorly drained: Water is removed so slowly that the soil is wet at shallow depths periodically during the growing season or remains wet for long periods. The occurrence of internal free water is shallow or very shallow and common or persistent. Free water is commonly at or near the surface long enough during the growing season so that most mesophytic crops cannot be grown, unless the soil is artificially drained. The soil, however, is not continuously wet directly below plow-depth. Free water at shallow depth is usually present. This water table is commonly the result of low or very low saturated hydraulic conductivity of nearly continuous rainfall, or of a combination of these.
classes.Very poorly drained: Water is removed from the soil so slowly that free water remains at or very near the ground surface during much of the growing season. The occurrence of internal free water is very shallow and persistent or permanent. Unless the soil is artificially drained, most mesophytic crops cannot be grown. The soils are commonly level or depressed and frequently ponded. If rainfall is high or nearly continuous, slope gradients may be greater.
classes.Subaqueous Soils: Free water is above the soil surface. Internal free water occurrence is permanent, and there is a positive water potential at the soil surface for more than 21 hours of each day. The soils have a peraquic soil moisture regime.

sec.name: OCsec
sec.title: Soil Erosion Class
sec.description: This layer displays the proportion of top soil lost to erosion for Orange County, California. It is derived from the US Department of Agriculture, Natural Resources Conservation Service SSURGO dataset, updated by Esri annually.
sec.phenomenon: Top soil loss due to erosion
sec.data_coordinate_system: WKID 5070 USA Contiguous Albers Equal Area Conic USGS version

classes.Class 1: In this map unit,1 to 25 percent of the original topsoil has been lost to erosion.
classes.Class 2: In this map unit, 25 to 75 percent of the original topsoil has been lost to erosion.
classes.Class 3: In this map unit, 75 to 99 percent of the original topsoil has been lost to erosion.
classes.Class 4: In this map unit, all of the original topsoil has been lost to erosion.

sltf.name: OCsltf
sltf.title: Soil Loss Tolerance Factor
sltf.description: This layer displays the maximum rate of erosion that will not result in a loss of crop productivity over time for Orange County, California. It is derived from the US Department of Agriculture, Natural Resources Conservation Service SSURGO dataset, updated by Esri annually.
sltf.data_coordinate_system: WKID 5070 USA Contiguous Albers Equal Area Conic USGS version

sffr.name: OCsffr
sffr.title: Soil Flooding Frequency
sffr.description: This layer provides an estimate of the likelihood of flooding in a given year for Orange County, California. It is derived from the US Department of Agriculture, Natural Resources Conservation Service SSURGO dataset, updated by Esri annually.
sffr.data_coordinate_system: WKID 5070 USA Contiguous Albers Equal Area Conic USGS version

classes.Very Frequent: Flooding is likely to occur very often under usual weather conditions; more than a 50 percent chance of flooding in all months of any year.
classes.Frequent: Flooding is likely to occur often under usual weather conditions; more than a 50 percent chance of flooding in any year (i.e., 50 times in 100 years), but less than a 50 percent chance of flooding in all months in any year.
classes.Common: (Obsolete Class) Combination of Occasional and Frequent
classes.Occasional: Flooding is expected infrequently under usual weather conditions; 5 to 50 percent chance of flooding in any year or 5 to 50 times in 100 years.
classes.Rare: Flooding is unlikely but is possible under unusual weather conditions; 1 to 5 percent chance of flooding in any year or nearly 1 to 5 times in 100 years.
classes.Very Rare: Flooding is very unlikely but is possible under extremely unusual weather conditions; less than 1 percent chance of flooding in any year or less than 1 time in 100 years but more than 1 time in 500 years.
classes.None: No reasonable possibility of flooding; one chance out of 500 of flooding in any year or less than 1 time in 500 years.
